steps.title: Understand what the question is asking
steps.content: The passage says Toomer describes Dixie Pike as having "a deep connection to a faraway place." We need a quotation that directly names and illustrates that faraway connection — not just any quotation about the road.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — "And when the wind is from the South, soil of my homeland falls like a fertile shower upon the lean streets of [Washington, DC].": Describes soil traveling to a northern city, but never mentions Dixie Pike or traces the road's origin to a faraway place. ✗
  • Choice B — "[Fern and I] walked down the [Dixie] Pike with people on all the porches gaping at us.": Depicts a local scene on the Pike; says nothing about a distant connection. ✗
  • Choice C — "The Dixie Pike has grown from a goat path in Africa.": Names the Pike explicitly and traces its origin to Africa — a faraway place — directly illustrating the claim. ✓
  • Choice D — "[The wagon] bumps, and groans, and shakes as it crosses the railroad track.": Sensory travel detail; no mention of the Pike's distant roots. ✗
That is the correct answer.
